query: association between past history of fetal abnormality and gestational diabetes mellitus

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) recommends targeted evaluation for type 2 diabetes early in pregnancy with a 75-g or 50-g oral glucose tolerance test at the initial prenatal visit in patients who have a BMI of 30 kg/m 2 or more and 1 of the following risk factors: History of gestational diabetes mellitus Hemoglobin A1C ≥5.7% on previous testing Immediate family member with diabetes High-risk race (eg, African American, Latin American, Native American, Asian American, Pacific Islander) Cardiovascular disease history Hypertension High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol level <35 mg/dL or a triglyceride level >250 mg/dL Polycystic ovary syndrome Physical inactivity [6] [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: ACOG recommends targeted evaluation for type 2 diabetes early in pregnancy with a 75-g or 50-g oral glucose tolerance test at the initial prenatal visit in patients who have a BMI of 30 kg/m 2 or more and 1 of the following risk factors: History of GDM Hemoglobin A1C ≥5.7% on previous testing Immediate family member with diabetes High-risk race (eg, African American, Latin American, Native American, Asian American, Pacific Islander) Cardiovascular disease history Hypertension High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol level <35 mg/dL or a triglyceride level >250 mg/dL Polycystic ovary syndrome Physical inactivity [12] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrical history: Preterm delivery, stillbirth, or a fetus affected by growth restriction, as well as any pregnancy complicated by preeclampsia, diabetes, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, or blood transfusion

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: GDM is primarily initially identified by screening tests in pregnancy. Because early screening is indicated by clinical history and assessment, documenting past medical history, obstetric outcomes,  and family history of type 2 diabetes mellitus are essential components of GDM assessment. The clinical features of gestational diabetes mellitus can be varied. The disproportionate weight gain, obesity, and elevated BMI can be suggestive features. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal history: Age, gravidity, parity, history of hypertension, diabetes, hypercoagulability, autoimmune disease, or cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A comprehensive maternal history and physical examination are essential components of prenatal care for women with diabetes. The history should carefully document the age of diabetes onset, the presence of diabetes-related complications, including diabetic ketoacidosis, hypoglycemia, gastroparesis, nephropathy, neuropathy, retinopathy, hypertension, and coronary artery disease, as well as any previous adverse pregnancy outcomes and abnormal glucose tolerance test results. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Prenatal laboratory test results: Complete blood count, type and screen, hepatitis B surface antigen, syphilis, human immunodeficiency virus, rubella, prenatal testing for aneuploidy, urine toxicology, and diabetic screening

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: However, studies have demonstrated that the most significant associations for GDM risk were family history, previous GDM, Southeast Asian ethnicity, parity, and high BMI. Therefore, ethnic and racial differences in these risk factors for GDM development should also be considered. [7] Additionally, recent studies have shown that early screening for gestational diabetes does not prevent adverse outcomes and may even lead to worse outcomes in select patients. These findings highlight the complexity of GDM pathogenesis and management. Consequently, the effectiveness of the early gestational diabetes screening recommendation is debatable and requires additional studies. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, recognizing early indicators of this condition is essential because GDM is a significant risk factor for macrosomia. Because early screening is indicated by clinical history and assessment, documenting past medical history, obstetric outcomes, and family history of type 2 diabetes are essential components of GDM assessment. The clinical features of GDM can be varied. The disproportionate weight gain, obesity, and elevated body mass index (BMI) can be suggestive features. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: A detailed history should be taken at the initial visit as well as in subsequent prenatal follow-up visits. Please see StatPearls' companion resources, " Initial Antepartum Care " and " Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester ," for more detailed information. In general, an obstetric history should include: The first day of the last menstrual period (LMP) and current estimated gestational age Parity Prepregnancy weight Immunization history Previous or preexisting medical conditions, including diabetes, obesity, polyhydramnios, Rh incompatibility Past pregnancies, including previous macrosomic infants' mode of delivery, associated complications, child gender

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Recommended first-trimester prenatal laboratory investigations include measurement of HbA1c, thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), a 24-hour urine collection (if baseline renal function is unavailable), and an electrocardiogram (ECG). HbA1c testing is crucial for detecting undiagnosed pregestational diabetes and establishing a baseline for glycemic control. The 24-hour urine collection assesses renal function, whereas the ECG screens for subclinical cardiovascular disease, which is more prevalent in pregnant women with diabetes. Thyroid function testing is imperative, given that up to 40% of young women with type 1 diabetes concurrently exhibit thyroid dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes Mellitus Screening -- Definition/Introduction -- Gestational Diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Gestational diabetes adversely impacts the lives of both fetus and mother, making screening for it an essential part of obstetric care. Screening is necessary for 1 of the risk factors for diabetes. In addition to the risk factors discussed above, others include: History of prediabetes (impaired glucose tolerance/fasting glucose, HbA1C>5.7) Prior history of gestational diabetes BMI>30 kg/m 2 Family history of diabetes Multiple gestations Old maternal age Glycosuria at the first prenatal visit History of unexplained miscarriage or malformation

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Current pregnancy: Abnormal uterine bleeding, trauma, reproductive treatment, exposure to drugs or radiation, weight gain, infections, sexually transmitted diseases, hypertension, preeclampsia, diabetes, anemia, fetal anomaly, or growth restriction

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Diabetes -- Etiology -- Gestational Diabetes Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: The following clinical factors are associated with a high risk of developing gestational diabetes: Increased body weight (a BMI >25 or >23 in Asian Americans) Decreased physical activity A first-degree relative with diabetes mellitus High-risk ethnicity (eg, African American, Latino, Native American, Asian American, Pacific Islander) Past history of any of the following GDM Newborn birth weight of ≥4000 g Cardiovascular diseases Hypertension Low HDL <35 mg/dL or a triglyceride level >250 mg/dL Polycystic ovarian syndrome Hemoglobin A1C >5.7 Abnormal oral glucose tolerance test Any significant marker of insulin resistance (eg, acanthosis nigricans) [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Innocent Murmur -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Neonates and Infants Conduct a perinatal, prenatal, as well as maternal history if paramount in the evaluation of newborns with heart murmurs. [1] Include: Maternal age, because advanced maternal age is associated with an increased risk of all heart defects. History of maternal infections, such as TORCH, with a special focus on rubella infection in the first trimester given its high association with patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), pulmonary valve abnormalities, peripheral pulmonary stenosis, and ventricular septal defects (VSD). Maternal diabetes mellitus is also of importance due to its association with asymmetric hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Oligohydramnios -- History and Physical -- Clinical History
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians reviewing the patient's medical history should look for conditions that may lead to maternal microvascular disease, eg, diabetic nephropathy or autoimmune disease. The family history can help determine a patient's risk of carrying a fetus with congenital anomalies and a genetic condition. The patient's medications should also be reviewed for possible causative agents.

retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل), threatened miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد). These associations highlight the importance of evaluating and managing metabolic, hematologic, and early‑pregnancy complications when counseling patients about elective pregnancy termination, ensuring comprehensive risk assessment and optimal maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1798
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل) encompass a range of conditions, including fetal disorder (اضطراب جنيني), threatened abortion (تهديد بالإجهاض), and gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل). Both threatened abortion and gestational diabetes are recognized as specific types of pregnancy complications. Clinically, optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is associated with the management of gestational diabetes and also with threatened abortion, indicating that termination may be considered as a therapeutic option in these particular obstetric scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2306
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. Among the many obstetric complications encompassed by this larger category, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium represents a specific type of complication. This relationship underscores that gestational diabetes and related disorders of glucose metabolism are recognized as important pregnancy‑related complications and are included in the overall spectrum of conditions that can affect maternal and fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period. Effective identification and management of these glucose‑related complications are essential components of comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that arises during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication within the broader category of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. This condition represents a distinct obstetric complication where altered glucose metabolism adversely affects maternal and fetal health, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing glucose levels throughout gestation, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1640
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal diabetes conditions and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are recognized as forms or manifestations of diabetes. In addition, the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is an inverse‑isa relationship to مرض السكري, meaning that gestational or peripartum diabetes represents a particular clinical presentation of the disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes), reflecting that patients with diabetes may also exhibit other pancreatic abnormalities unrelated to the primary diabetic process. Moreover, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (a retinal disorder) and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), underscoring the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse forms of diabetes—including transient neonatal types and pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance—and their broader systemic associations with pancreatic and retinal pathology, which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 671
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease entity that connects a wide array of metabolic, developmental, obstetric, ophthalmic, musculoskeletal, infectious and pharmacologic concepts. All recognized forms of neonatal diabetes—including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal 1, and the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes—are mapped to مرض السكري, underscoring its relevance in early‑life glucose dysregulation. In the obstetric domain, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that complicates gestation, childbirth or the puerperium is classified as an inverse subclass of مرض السكري, highlighting maternal‑fetal risk. Norgestrel is explicitly contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, reflecting an important drug‑disease interaction for contraceptive planning. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is recorded as a child of مرض السكري, and both entities share the comorbidity label

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the broader condition مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). These specific neonatal and developmental presentations are therefore recognized as manifestations or subtypes of diabetes mellitus. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, indicating that retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy commonly occur in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians identify atypical early‑onset forms of diabetes and monitor for ocular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all recognized as specific forms or presentations that map to مرض السكري (diabetes). The condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is also identified as a manifestation of مرض السكري, emphasizing the impact of diabetes on maternal and perinatal health. Norgestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that this contraceptive medication should be avoided in individuals with the disease. Additionally, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the diverse neonatal and developmental expressions of diabetes, its complications during pregnancy, medication safety considerations, and its association with retinal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2061
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disorder (اضطراب جنيني), premature rupture of membranes (تمزق الأغشية الباكر), threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر) and gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل) are all classified as pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل). Both gestational diabetes and premature rupture of membranes are clinically associated with the use of a low transverse cesarean section, indicating that patients with these conditions may be more likely to require this specific surgical delivery approach. Recognizing these obstetric conditions as part of the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians anticipate appropriate interventions, such as low transverse cesarean section, and tailor management to improve maternal and fetal outcomes.
